title: The iterator protocol
instruction:
Before generators, let's understand iterators. An **iterator** is any object with a `next()` method that returns `{ value, done }`.

`for...of` works on any iterator — it calls `next()` repeatedly until `done: true`.

Run this to see the protocol.
successMessage: The iterator protocol: next() returns {value, done}. done: true signals the end. for...of calls next() automatically until done is true. Generators automate this boilerplate.

title: Generator functions with yield
instruction:
`function*` (note the asterisk) creates a generator. `yield` pauses it and emits a value.

Callers use `next()` to resume.

Run this to see the generator pause and resume.
successMessage: Same protocol, less code! The generator handles the {value, done} wrapping automatically. yield pauses execution and sends the value to the caller.

title: Generators work with for...of
instruction:
Generators implement the iterable protocol automatically — they work directly in `for...of`, spread, and destructuring.

No need to call `next()` manually.
successMessage: Generators are first-class iterables. Spread, for...of, destructuring — they all work. range(0, 1000000) is memory-efficient because values are produced one at a time.

title: Infinite generators
instruction:
Generators can be infinite — they never run out of values. You take as many as you need.

This is safe because values are produced lazily — the generator doesn't compute them all upfront.
hint: function* naturals() { let n = 1; while (true) { yield n++; } }
successMessage: Infinite generator! It never ends, but take() breaks early. This is how database cursors work — they can scroll through billions of rows without loading them all.

title: yield* — delegate to another generator
instruction:
`yield*` delegates to another iterable — generator, array, string, anything iterable.

It's like spreading one generator inside another.
successMessage: yield* delegates iteration. Combined flattened both generators and an array into one sequence. This is how you compose generators into pipelines.

title: Two-way communication with generators
instruction:
Generators can receive values through `next(value)`. The `yield` expression evaluates to that value:

```js
const input = yield "prompt";  // receives value from next(value)
```

This makes generators two-way communication channels.
successMessage: Two-way communication through yield. Redux-Saga uses this to manage complex async flows — each yield is a point where saga middleware injects a result.

title: Custom iterable class
instruction:
Make any class iterable by implementing `[Symbol.iterator]()` — a method that returns an iterator.

Once you do this, `for...of`, spread, and destructuring all work on your class.
successMessage: [Symbol.iterator]() is the magic method. Any object that has it becomes iterable. Spread, for...of, destructuring — they all call it. This is how custom collections work.

title: Lazy pipeline with generators
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a lazy data pipeline using generators:
- `function* map(iterable, fn)` — lazy map
- `function* filter(iterable, fn)` — lazy filter
- `function* take(iterable, n)` — take first n items

Then chain them to process the naturals sequence (from step 4): take the first 5 even numbers and double them.

Do this without creating any intermediate arrays.
successMessage: A lazy functional pipeline! No intermediate arrays — values flow from naturals through filter and map one at a time, then take stops after 5. Chapter 19 done!
